title: From Corporate Law to Fighting Human Trafficking: One Woman's Bold Career Pivot
guestBio: Leigh Dundas is a human rights attorney, modern-day abolitionist, and award-winning public speaker who transitioned from Fortune 500 corporate litigation to leading undercover missions against child trafficking networks in Southeast Asia. She has become a powerful advocate for civil liberties and medical freedom in the United States.
excerpt: Human rights attorney Leigh Dundas shares her extraordinary journey from high-stakes corporate litigation to leading dangerous undercover missions against child trafficking. Her story will redefine what you think courage and leadership really look like.
metaDescription: Human rights attorney Leigh Dundas reveals her bold career shift from corporate law to fighting child trafficking and defending civil liberties.
keyTakeaways: True leadership often requires making difficult career pivots that align with your deeper purpose and values.
Grassroots movements can be incredibly powerful when individuals are willing to take bold action for what they believe in.
Managing the emotional toll of advocacy work requires intentional boundaries and self-care strategies.
Standing up for civil liberties and human rights often means facing significant personal and professional risks.
Corporate experience can provide valuable skills that translate powerfully into human rights advocacy work.
Courage isn't the absence of fear, but taking action despite being afraid.
Individual voices can create massive change when backed by conviction and strategic action.

pullQuotes.text: Sometimes the most important work is the work that nobody wants to talk about.
Leadership isn't about having a title - it's about being willing to do what others won't.
The skills I learned in corporate litigation prepared me for fights I never imagined I'd be having.

timestamps.label: Fighting for medical freedom and civil liberties
Managing the emotional toll of advocacy work
Final thoughts and call to action

content:
What does it take to walk away from a successful corporate law career to fight some of the world's most dangerous battles? In this powerful episode of The Manage Her, host Aimee Rickabus sits down with human rights attorney Leigh Dundas to explore a career transformation that will challenge everything you think you know about courage, purpose, and leadership.

Leigh's story isn't your typical career pivot narrative. This is a woman who went from representing Fortune 500 companies in high-stakes litigation to leading undercover missions against child trafficking networks in Southeast Asia. More recently, she's become a fierce advocate for civil liberties and medical freedom right here in the United States. Her journey reveals the extraordinary power that lies within each of us when we align our skills with our deepest convictions.

The Courage to Pivot When Purpose Calls

For many women in leadership, there comes a moment when success in the traditional sense no longer feels like enough. Leigh experienced this awakening during her corporate law career, realizing that her litigation skills could be weaponized for something far more meaningful than corporate disputes. The transition wasn't easy, and it certainly wasn't safe, but it was necessary.

What makes Leigh's story so compelling is how she leveraged her existing expertise rather than starting from scratch. The same skills that made her effective in boardrooms became her secret weapons in fighting human trafficking. Strategic thinking, negotiation tactics, understanding complex legal frameworks, and maintaining composure under pressure all translated directly into her human rights work.

This teaches us something crucial about career pivots: you don't have to throw away everything you've built. Instead, you can redirect your accumulated expertise toward work that ignites your soul. The question isn't whether you're qualified to make a change, but whether you're willing to use what you already know in service of something bigger.

Leadership in the Shadows

Perhaps the most striking aspect of Leigh's work is how much of it happens completely out of public view. Her undercover operations in Southeast Asia required a kind of leadership that looks nothing like what we typically celebrate. There were no awards, no public recognition, and certainly no social media moments. Just dangerous, necessary work done in the shadows to protect the most vulnerable.

This challenges our modern obsession with visible leadership. Real impact often happens where no one is watching, where there's no applause, and where the only reward is knowing you've made a difference. Leigh's experience reminds us that some of the most important leadership work is invisible labor that requires immense personal sacrifice.

The lessons here extend far beyond human rights advocacy. Whether you're building a business, leading a team, or creating change in your community, the most transformative work often happens behind the scenes. It's the late nights, the difficult conversations, the unglamorous tasks that nobody sees but everyone benefits from.

Managing the Emotional Toll

One of the most honest parts of this conversation centers on how Leigh manages the emotional weight of her work. Fighting child trafficking and defending civil liberties means constant exposure to humanity's darkest realities. It's work that can consume you if you don't have strong boundaries and support systems.

Leigh's approach to self-care isn't about bubble baths and wine nights. It's about creating sustainable practices that allow her to continue showing up for work that matters. This includes everything from spiritual practices to intentional time away from the work, and building a support network of people who understand the unique challenges of advocacy work.

For any woman doing meaningful but heavy work, whether it's running a business that serves others, working in healthcare, education, or social services, Leigh's strategies offer a roadmap. You cannot pour from an empty cup, and sustaining yourself isn't selfish—it's essential for long-term impact.

The Power of Individual Action

Throughout their conversation, both Aimee and Leigh emphasize something that can be easy to forget in our current political and social climate: individual action still matters enormously. Grassroots movements built by people willing to take personal risks for their convictions can create massive systemic change.

Leigh's recent work defending medical freedom and civil liberties demonstrates how one person's willingness to speak up can inspire others to find their own voices. She didn't wait for permission or for someone else to lead the charge. She saw a need and stepped into the gap, using her platform and expertise to advocate for principles she believes in.

This is perhaps the most empowering message for listeners: you don't need a special invitation to become a leader in the areas that matter to you. Whether it's in your industry, your community, or on issues that affect your family, your voice and your action can be the catalyst that creates the change you want to see.

Redefining Success and Impact

Leigh's journey forces us to reconsider what success really looks like. In a culture obsessed with metrics, visibility, and external validation, her work offers a different model. Success isn't always about growth charts and profit margins. Sometimes it's about lives saved, freedoms preserved, and injustices confronted.

This doesn't mean abandoning ambition or professional achievement. Rather, it's about expanding our definition of what it means to build a meaningful career. For women who feel pulled toward work that serves others, Leigh's example provides both inspiration and practical guidance for making that transition without losing everything you've worked to build.

As this powerful conversation concludes, one thing becomes crystal clear: we all have more power than we realize to create change, fight for what matters, and lead from wherever we are. Leigh Dundas didn't wait for perfect conditions or complete preparation. She saw problems that needed solving and used the tools at her disposal to solve them. Her story is a reminder that sometimes the most extraordinary leadership begins with simply deciding that you can't stand by and do nothing anymore.
